name: Word Patterns — Spotting How Korean Works

cards.grammarNote: 공부 (gongbu) = study (noun) + 하다 = to do → 공부하다 = to study. Thousands of Sino-Korean nouns follow this pattern. Polite form: 공부해요 (gongbuhaeyo).
사랑 (sarang) = love (noun) → 사랑하다 = to love. Polite: 사랑해요 (saranghaeyo). One of the most common -하다 verbs.
청소 (cheongso) = cleaning (noun) → 청소하다 = to clean. Polite: 청소해요 (cheongsohaeyo). Any Sino-Korean noun can potentially take 하다.
사회 (sahoe) = society → 사회적인 = social. The suffix -적인 (jeokin) works like English -ic or -al. Use 적인 before a noun; use 적으로 (jeogeuro) to form an adverb: 사회적으로 = socially.
개인 (gaein) = individual → 개인적인 = personal / individual. Example: 개인적인 질문이에요 (gaeinjeokin jilmunieyo) = It's a personal question.
불- (bul-) + 가능 (ganeung, possible) = 불가능 = impossible. 불- is the most common negative prefix in Korean everyday speech. Compare: 불행 (bulhaeng) = unhappiness, 불편 (bulpyeon) = inconvenient.
비- (bi-) + 공식 (gongsik, official) = 비공식 = unofficial. 비- means non- or un- and is common in formal and news contexts. Also: 비공개 (bigonggae) = private / undisclosed.
무- (mu-) + 조건 (jogeon, condition) = 무조건 = unconditional / no matter what. 무- conveys total absence. Also: 무관심 (mugwansim) = indifference, 무한 (muhan) = infinite.

cards.front: 먹기 is the noun form of ___
cards.grammarNote: 먹다 (meokda, to eat) → drop 다, add 기 → 먹기 (meokgi) = eating. The -기 suffix turns any verb into a gerund noun. Used in: 먹기 좋아해요 = I like eating.
말하다 (malhada, to speak) → 말하기 (malhagi, speaking) + 가 [subject] + 어려워요 (eoryeowoyo, is difficult). -기 makes the verb the subject of the sentence.
가다 (gada, to go) → stem 가 + ㄹ 수 있어요 = 갈 수 있어요. Vowel stems add -ㄹ; consonant stems add -을. 갈 수 없어요 (gal su eopseoyo) = I can't go.
먹다 (meokda, to eat) → stem 먹 ends in consonant → add 을 수 없어요. Pattern: consonant stem + 을 수 있다/없다. 먹을 수 있어요 = I can eat it.
하다 (hada, to do) → stem 하 (vowel) + ㄹ 수 있어요 = 할 수 있어요. A common expression of encouragement in Korean. Negative: 할 수 없어요 = I can't do it.
